title: Technology in the Classroom
prompt: Some people believe technology improves classroom learning, while others think it distracts students. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
sampleEssay:
Digital technology has become a normal part of education, yet its role in the classroom remains debated. Supporters argue that technological integration gives learners faster access to knowledge and more engaging ways to practise. Critics, however, worry that screens weaken concentration and encourage superficial learning. In my view, technology improves pedagogical effectiveness when teachers use it for a clear academic purpose rather than as a substitute for instruction.

On the positive side, digital tools can facilitate cognitive development by making difficult ideas easier to understand. For example, an interactive science simulation allows students to observe processes that cannot be reproduced safely in a school laboratory. Online quizzes also provide immediate feedback, helping learners identify mistakes before an examination. Furthermore, recorded lessons can support students who need to review a topic at a slower pace, thereby strengthening academic achievement.

Nevertheless, unrestricted device use can create digital dependency and other detrimental consequences. Notifications, games, and social media compete for attention, while copying information from the internet may undermine independent thinking. Schools therefore need practical rules. Devices should be used during planned activities, unnecessary applications should be blocked, and students should still complete discussion, handwriting, and problem-solving tasks without screens. These safeguards are fundamental because innovation without discipline can reduce rather than improve learning quality.

In conclusion, technology is neither automatically beneficial nor harmful. Its educational value depends on how it is selected and supervised. A sustainable classroom model should combine skilled teaching, focused digital activities, and traditional learning methods. Such a balanced approach can produce substantial benefits while protecting concentration, intellectual independence, and long-term academic progress.

title: Investment in Public Transport
prompt: Governments should spend more money on public transport than on building new roads. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
sampleEssay:
Growing cities must decide whether limited public funds should support public transport or additional roads. Although roads remain fundamental for deliveries and emergency services, I largely agree that governments should give greater priority to reliable buses, railways, and metro systems. A sustainable transport network can move considerably more people while reducing congestion, pollution, and unequal access to employment.

The strongest argument for public transport is efficiency. A full bus or train carries many passengers in far less space than the same number of private cars. When services are frequent, safe, and affordable, commuters have a realistic alternative to driving. This can shorten travel times for everyone, including people who must continue using roads. Public transport can also facilitate access to education and employment, producing significant social and economic benefits for citizens who cannot afford a car.

Environmental benefits are equally substantial. Fewer individual vehicles mean lower fuel consumption and cleaner urban air. Investment can support electric buses and modern rail networks, helping cities meet long-term climate targets. By contrast, new roads often provide only temporary relief because easier driving inevitably encourages additional traffic. This pattern may create detrimental health consequences and higher infrastructure costs.

Nevertheless, road maintenance should not be neglected. Rural communities and commercial transport depend predominantly on safe connections, while poorly designed public systems cannot serve every journey. Governments should therefore preserve essential roads without allowing continuous expansion to undermine investment in shared networks.

Overall, prioritising public transport is the more sustainable and inclusive policy. It addresses congestion at its source, strengthens urban mobility, and gives a larger proportion of the population dependable access to opportunity.

title: Remote Work and Productivity
prompt: Working from home is becoming more common. Do the advantages of remote work outweigh the disadvantages?
sampleEssay:
Remote work has moved from a limited employment benefit to a common arrangement in many industries. It offers substantial flexibility and saves commuting time, but it can also reduce collaboration and blur the boundary between professional and personal life. I believe the advantages outweigh the disadvantages when organisations establish clear expectations, sustainable routines, and consistent communication.

The main benefit is that employees can use their time more efficiently. A person who no longer spends two hours travelling may begin work with greater energy or use that time for family responsibilities. Employers can also recruit skilled people outside their immediate city and reduce office costs. For tasks requiring concentration, a quiet home environment may facilitate deeper analysis and produce significant improvements in productivity.

On the other hand, remote employees may feel isolated, and quick discussions can become scheduled meetings. New staff can find it difficult to learn workplace habits without observing experienced colleagues. Furthermore, some homes lack suitable space or reliable internet access. These detrimental conditions may undermine performance and wellbeing, particularly when managers provide insufficient support.

Nevertheless, these disadvantages can be reduced through hybrid schedules, equipment assistance, planned mentoring, and sensible limits on after-hours messages. Remote work is most successful when flexibility is supported by structure. Teams need measurable goals, shared working hours, secure technological integration, and opportunities for face-to-face contact when complex collaboration requires it.

In conclusion, working from home provides considerable benefits for employees and organisations. Although poor management can create digital dependency or isolation, these risks are manageable. The gains in flexibility, access to talent, productive time, and organisational resilience remain fundamental reasons to preserve remote work as a long-term option.

title: Happiness and Economic Success
prompt: Happiness affects personal economic success. Do you agree with this? Give your own example.
sampleEssay:
In the modern world, the connection between psychological wellbeing and financial prosperity has become a subject of considerable debate. While traditional views suggest that wealth generates happiness, I strongly agree that happiness can also drive personal economic success. A positive state of mind can improve professional performance, strengthen relationships, and support better long-term decisions.

To begin with, happy individuals often demonstrate greater motivation and productivity at work. Positive emotions can improve creative problem-solving and help employees respond calmly to pressure. Consequently, they may complete tasks more effectively, cooperate with colleagues, and remain persistent after setbacks. These qualities can lead to promotions, performance bonuses, and more secure employment.

Furthermore, happiness can strengthen professional networks. Cheerful and emotionally stable people often communicate confidently with managers, clients, and business partners. For example, consider two real-estate agents with similar technical knowledge. An anxious agent may struggle to build trust, whereas an enthusiastic agent is more likely to understand buyers, maintain useful contacts, and complete profitable transactions. Emotional wellbeing can therefore produce measurable financial advantages.

Admittedly, happiness alone cannot guarantee prosperity. Education, opportunity, discipline, and access to resources remain important. Nevertheless, people who protect their health and maintain constructive routines are often better prepared to use these opportunities wisely.

In conclusion, happiness is not merely a reward for financial stability; it can be a foundation for achieving it. By improving productivity, resilience, and social interaction, a positive mindset can make economic success more attainable and sustainable.

title: University for Every Young Person
prompt: Some people believe every young person should go to university. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
sampleEssay:
University education offers valuable knowledge and professional preparation, yet it should not be treated as the only respectable path for every young person. Although higher education is essential for careers such as medicine, engineering, and teaching, I disagree that all school leavers must attend university because abilities, ambitions, and labour-market needs differ considerably.

Universities can develop critical thinking, subject expertise, and communication skills. Students also gain access to laboratories, academic mentors, and professional networks that may be difficult to find independently. For occupations requiring regulated qualifications, a degree is fundamental. Graduates may consequently enjoy stronger employment prospects and contribute to research and public services.

Nevertheless, many young people are better suited to vocational education, apprenticeships, entrepreneurship, or creative work. A talented electrician, software developer, designer, or chef can build a sustainable career through focused practical training. Requiring such learners to complete an expensive academic programme may create debt without improving their employment prospects. It can also produce shortages in skilled trades that communities depend upon.

Admittedly, non-university routes still require discipline and high-quality instruction. Governments should therefore ensure that technical colleges and apprenticeships meet credible standards rather than presenting them as easier alternatives. Career guidance should help students compare costs, outcomes, and personal strengths.

In conclusion, university remains a powerful route to knowledge and opportunity, but it is not universally appropriate. Young people should choose the pathway that best matches their abilities and goals, while society should value academic and vocational achievement equally.
